full_response:
Let  

 T         = the unique minimum-spanning tree (MST) of G (weights are distinct),  
 w(T)    = total weight of T,  
 E\T    = the set of edges that do not belong to T.  

For e = (x,y) ∈ E\T let P_T(x,y) be the unique x–y path that T already contains and let  
 m(e) = the heaviest edge on P_T(x,y).  
Because T is an MST and the weights are distinct, the cut property implies  

 w(m(e)) < w(e)                                        (1)

If we add e to T we create the cycle C = P_T(x,y) ∪ {e}.  
Removing m(e) from that cycle yields the spanning tree  

 T_e = T – {m(e)} ∪ {e}.                              (2)

Its weight is  

 w(T_e) = w(T) + (w(e) – w(m(e))) > w(T).             (3)

Thus every non-tree edge produces a tree that differs from T in exactly one edge and is (strictly) heavier than T.

Claim 1 (Existence).  
Let ê be the edge that minimises the positive increment in (3):

 ê = argmin_{e∈E\T} [w(e) – w(m(e))].

Then T_{ê} is a second-best minimum-spanning tree.

Proof.  
For any spanning tree S ≠ T we will show w(S) ≥ w(T_{ê}).  
Consider D = (T \ S) ≠ ∅.  Pick an edge f ∈ D.  
Adding f to S forms a unique cycle; because of (1) that cycle contains an edge g ∈ S\T whose weight satisfies w(g) > w(f).  
Define S' = S – {g} ∪ {f}.  Then  

 w(S') = w(S) – w(g) + w(f) < w(S)                (4)

and |S' \ T| = |S \ T| – 1.  
Repeating the argument while |S' \ T| ≥ 2 strictly decreases the weight at every step, so after at most |V|–1 iterations we obtain a tree S* that

 (i) differs from T in exactly one edge, and  
 (ii) satisfies w(S*) ≤ w(S).

But every tree that differs from T in one edge has the form T_e for some e ∈ E\T, hence

 w(S) ≥ w(S*) = w(T_e) ≥ w(T_{ê}).               (5)

Because S was arbitrary, T_{ê} has weight no larger than any spanning tree other than T; by (3) its weight is larger than w(T), so it is the (unique) second-best MST. ∎



Claim 2 (Uniqueness of the 1-edge change).  
Every second-best MST differs from the MST in exactly one edge.

Proof.  
Assume, for contradiction, that an optimal second-best tree S differs from T in k ≥ 2 edges.  
Apply the transformation of (4) once to obtain S' with |S' \ T| = k–1 and w(S') < w(S), contradicting optimality.  
Therefore k = 1. ∎



Consequences.  
1.  The set {T_e : e ∈ E\T} contains every second-best MST.  
2.  The second-best cost is  

 w(T) + min_{e∈E\T}(w(e) – w(m(e))).



(An O(E log V) algorithm follows directly: build the MST once, preprocess the tree for “maximum-edge-on-path” queries, and scan every non-tree edge.)

\boxed{\text{A spanning tree is a second-best MST iff it is obtained from the (unique) MST by exchanging exactly one of its edges with a single non-tree edge that closes the corresponding fundamental cycle.}}

Confidence: 83%
extracted_answer: \text{A spanning tree is a second-best MST iff it is obtained from the (unique) MST by exchanging exactly one of its edges with a single non-tree edge that closes the corresponding fundamental cycle.}
